step_by_step_thinking: \boxed{B} Epidural anesthesia does not affect the Apgar score of the infant. The evidence specifically cites a large meta-analysis showing that rates of 5-minute APGAR scores less than 7 were similar regardless of when pushing began—this indicates stability in neonatal outcomes—including Apgar scoring—and supports the conclusion that epidural anesthesia does not negatively impact neonatal assessment metrics like the Apgar score. Additionally, regional anesthesia is shown to reduce fetal drug exposure and improve maternal safety through better hemodynamics, reinforcing its neutrality on newborn outcomes. Thus, epidural analgesia is associated with stable neonatal outcomes.
raw_response:
\boxed{B} Epidural anesthesia does not affect the Apgar score of the infant. The evidence specifically cites a large meta-analysis showing that rates of 5-minute APGAR scores less than 7 were similar regardless of when pushing began—this indicates stability in neonatal outcomes—including Apgar scoring—and supports the conclusion that epidural anesthesia does not negatively impact neonatal assessment metrics like the Apgar score. Additionally, regional anesthesia is shown to reduce fetal drug exposure and improve maternal safety through better hemodynamics, reinforcing its neutrality on newborn outcomes. Thus, epidural analgesia is associated with stable neonatal outcomes.
\boxed{B}
